confounders: Obstructive sleep apnea with intermittent CPAP adherence causing hypoxemia and secondary erythrocytosis

furosemide 20 mg PO daily (if volume overloaded), titrate to euvolemia
non_pharmacological: Refer to CTEPH center for pulmonary endarterectomy evaluation

Supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO2 >= 90% if hypoxemic
Vaccinations (influenza, pneumococcal) and avoid pregnancy; contraception counseling if relevant

Open with: “I can’t shake this cough and I’m getting out of breath doing normal stuff.” Volunteer early: dry cough most days x6 weeks, exertional shortness of breath (stairs/groceries), fatigue, evening low-grade fevers, occasional chest tightness when coughing, and that a recent chest scan showed “hazy patches/ground-glass areas.” If asked, add: started after a bad cold; night sweats a few nights/week; ~8 lb unintentional weight loss with low appetite; mostly dry cough with occasional clear mucus; worse with deep breaths; two antibiotic courses didn’t help; a borrowed inhaler didn’t help; no blood.
Withhold unless specifically asked: home pulse-ox drops with activity but normal at rest; unusually winded after shower/chores; new achy joints/muscle soreness; occasional bluish fingertips; a new medication started 2 months ago.
Be reluctant to disclose THC vaping and using someone else’s leftover steroid pills; admit only if asked directly, worried about judgment. Express misconceptions: think it might be asthma, stubborn pneumonia needing stronger antibiotics, reflux, or lung cancer. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, share fears about medication/scan costs and missing work, and ask about affordable options.

GERD with possible microaspiration contributing to ground-glass changes

required_evidence: HRCT ground-glass opacities with organizing pneumonia pattern
Low procalcitonin and no clear bacterial source

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a cooperative 9-year-old boy. Speak clearly in simple words; your parent is present and answers cost/medication questions. Start with: “I keep getting bad skin infections and chest infections over and over.” If not interrupted, volunteer: painful swollen skin bumps that sometimes fill with pus; a cough that keeps coming back with thick mucus; you’ve needed antibiotics a lot this past year.

Only if asked, add: fevers with skin flares, shortness of breath when sick (worse on stairs), daily itchiness, mouth sores/thrush a few times, and more than one pneumonia.

Do not mention unless specifically probed about history: infections since little, cuts/bug bites get infected easily, sometimes don’t feel very sick even when it’s bad, and scars fade slowly.

If asked about rashes in folds/groin, be a bit reluctant but admit itchy raw rash there.

Express misconceptions: you think it might be “MRSA,” or allergies/asthma because you wheeze with colds, or diabetes due to frequent infections.

If clinician shows empathy, respond with relief and openness. Emphasize family worries about costs, using leftover antibiotics, and avoiding specialists unless very sick.
